mission.title: Install and Configure Prometheus Operator / kube-prometheus-stack
mission.description: The Prometheus Operator simplifies the deployment and management of Prometheus monitoring stacks on Kubernetes using custom resources. It is ideal for users looking to automate monitoring configurations and manage Prometheus instances seamlessly. Key trade-offs include the need for familiarity with Kubernetes custom resources and potential complexity in advanced configurations.

steps.title: Add the Prometheus Operator Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Prometheus Operator Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts

helm repo update

```

> This is the official repository for Prometheus Operator.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the Prometheus Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the Prometheus Operator using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace monitoring

helm install prometheus-operator prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --version 0.75.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the monitoring stack.
> Installs the kube-prometheus-stack with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks that the Prometheus Operator and its components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n monitoring

kubectl get svc -n monitoring

```

> Lists all pods in the monitoring namespace to verify they are running.
> Lists all services in the monitoring namespace to ensure they are created.

steps.title: Create an example Prometheus resource
steps.description:
This command creates a sample Prometheus custom resource to start monitoring your applications.

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: Prometheus
metadata:
  name: example-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  replicas: 1
  serviceAccountName: prometheus-operator
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 400Mi
EOF

```

> Creates a basic Prometheus instance.
commands:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: Prometheus
metadata:
  name: example-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:

  resources:

      memory: 400Mi
EOF

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Prometheus Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Prometheus Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all monitoring data and configurations.

```bash

helm uninstall prometheus-operator --namespace monitoring

kubectl delete namespace monitoring

```

> Uninstalls the Prometheus Operator.
> Deletes the monitoring namespace and all its resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Prometheus Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your Prometheus data and configurations. This step will upgrade the Prometheus Operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl get prometheus -n monitoring -o yaml > prometheus-backup.yaml

helm upgrade prometheus-operator prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --version 0.77.0

kubectl get prometheus -n monitoring

```

> Backup the current Prometheus configuration.
> Upgrades the Prometheus Operator to the specified version.
> Verify that the Prometheus instance is running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get prometheus -n monitoring -o yaml > prometheus-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error creating custom resource definition'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the CRD is not installed or there are permission issues.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the CRDs are installed and that you have the necessary permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Prometheus pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to misconfiguration or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Prometheus pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n monitoring' and adjust the resource requests.

troubleshooting.title: Service not found error when accessing Prometheus UI
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate that the Prometheus service is not properly created.

**Fix:**
Run 'kubectl get svc -n monitoring' to verify the service exists and check the service type.

troubleshooting.title: Alertmanager not receiving alerts
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This could be due to misconfigured alert rules or lack of targets.

**Fix:**
Verify alert rules and ensure that targets are correctly configured in Prometheus.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new alerting rules and enhanced Grafana dashboards.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Review and update any deprecated alerting rules in your configurations.

resolution.summary: You should see the Prometheus UI accessible at 'http://<prometheus-service-ip>:9090' and the status of Prometheus should show 'UP'.

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
